expectations.text: Decomposes into 4 work units with no dependencies between them
expectations.evidence: Trace: 'Would Claude decompose into work units with user confirmation? NO. No work plan presentation.' Summary: 'Work decomposition: NO — All endpoints handled as monolithic feature.' Step 3 shows all 4 endpoints written sequentially in a single pass, not decomposed into independent units.

expectations.text: Computes a single parallel batch containing all 4 units
expectations.evidence: Trace: 'Would Claude detect that endpoints are independent? NO. The TDD skill's Phase 2 performs dependency analysis and explicitly identifies independent work units.' No batch computation occurs. Summary: 'Dependency analysis: NO — Independence not formally identified.'

expectations.text: Presents the execution plan showing all units run concurrently in Batch 1
expectations.evidence: No execution plan is presented. Trace: 'No work plan presentation. No user confirmation of decomposition. Claude decides the structure and proceeds.' Summary: 'User confirmation of plan: NO — Structure decided unilaterally.'

expectations.text: Dispatches multiple Test Writers simultaneously (up to maxParallelPairs config, default 4)
expectations.evidence: Trace: 'Would Claude use agent teams? NO. Single-session execution. This is the eval where the performance difference is most dramatic.' No Test Writers exist, let alone multiple simultaneous ones. Summary: 'Agent teams: NO.'

expectations.text: Each unit independently goes through the full RED->GREEN->review cycle
expectations.evidence: Trace: 'No RED/GREEN verification: For any endpoint.' Summary: 'RED verification: NO. GREEN verification: NO. Formal reviews: NO.' No unit-level TDD cycles exist. All endpoints implemented first, then tests written to match.

expectations.text: Applies the three-stage review pipeline: spec compliance -> adversarial -> code quality
expectations.evidence: Trace: 'Would Claude do formal reviews? NO. No adversarial review, no spec compliance review, no code quality review. Each endpoint gets zero independent validation.' Summary: 'Formal reviews: NO — No per-endpoint adversarial/spec/quality review.'

expectations.text: Runs a final integration test after all units complete
expectations.evidence: Trace: 'Would Claude run a final integration review? WEAKLY. Claude might run all tests together at the end, but there's no formal Phase 5 with: Full test suite execution with actual output reading, Cross-unit integration check.' Summary: 'Integration review: WEAK.' While tests may be run, there is no formal integration review step.

claims: Baseline serializes 4 independent endpoints through a single session — no parallel execution
No work unit decomposition despite user explicitly saying 'independent endpoints'
No agent teams: all code and tests written by the same session in sequence
No per-endpoint TDD cycle (RED, GREEN, reviews) — all endpoints handled monolithically
Performance ceiling: potentially 4x slower than parallel TDD approach for fully independent work
No execution plan presentation or user confirmation of decomposition strategy

suggestions: Assertion 7 (integration test) is borderline: baseline does run tests at the end, but without formal Phase 5 verification. The grading correctly requires formal integration review, not just running tests.
This eval most clearly demonstrates the throughput advantage of agent teams. Consider measuring actual wall-clock time in live runs to quantify the 4x speedup claim.
Future evals could test with 8 independent units against maxParallelPairs=4 to see if baseline's sequential approach becomes even more disadvantaged.
eval_feedback.overall: The baseline fails all 7 assertions for the parallel batch eval. This is the eval where the throughput gap is largest: 4 fully independent endpoints that could run in parallel are serialized through a single session. No decomposition, no agent teams, no per-endpoint verification, and no formal integration review. The baseline's single-session architecture fundamentally cannot exploit the parallelism that the skill's agent teams enable.
